request.question: Which English actor, born in 1957 and considered a method actor, starred in the 2009 musical drama film "Nine."

title: Aaron Sidwell
Alexander Fiske-Harrison
Hugo Johnstone-Burt
Daniel Day-Lewis
Michael Ironside
Christian Bale filmography
Falling Awake (film)
Rhythm (2000 film)
Nine (2009 live-action film)
Dilip Kumar
sentences: Aaron Michael Sidwell (born 12 September 1988) is an English actor and singer .

He is best known for playing villain Steven Beale in the British soap opera EastEnders, originating the role of Michael Dork in Loserville in the West End and fronting his own pop group, The Boston Plan.
He was also the first British actor to play Johnny in Green Day's American Idiot musical.
Sidwell is a method actor, known for using Stanislavski technique
Alexander Rupert Fiske-Harrison (born 22 July 1976) is an English prize-winning author and journalist, broadcaster and conservationist.
His writing has been noted for his immersion in his subject matter.
He also trained and worked for some years as a Method actor.
Hugo Johnstone-Burt (born 1987/1988) is a Scottish-born Australian actor.
He grew up in Sydney and decided to become an actor after attending his first drama class.
Johnstone-Burt graduated from the National Institute of Dramatic Art in 2009.
He appeared in small roles in Australian dramas "Rake", "Sea Patrol" and "", before he was cast as Fish Lamb in "Cloudstreet".
The role earned him two ASTRA Award nominations.
Johnstone-Burt went on to star in "Carelesss Love" and he took on the role of Constable Hugh Collins in ABC1's "Miss Fisher's Murder Mysteries".
In 2012, Johnstone-Burt appeared in "Tricky Business" and filmed a guest role in "Home and Away".
He has also appeared in the musical drama film "Goddess" (2013) and the disaster film "San Andreas" (2015).
Sir Daniel Michael Blake Day-Lewis (born 29 April 1957) is an English actor who holds both British and Irish citizenship.
Born and raised in London, he excelled on stage at the National Youth Theatre, before being accepted at the Bristol Old Vic Theatre School, which he attended for three years.
Despite his traditional actor training at the Bristol Old Vic, he is considered to be a method actor, known for his constant devotion to and research of his roles.
He would often remain completely in character for the duration of the shooting schedules of his films, even to the point of adversely affecting his health.
He is one of the most selective actors in the film industry, having starred in only five films since 1998, with as many as five years between roles.
Protective of his private life, he rarely gives interviews and makes very few public appearances.
Frederick Reginald Ironside (born February 12, 1950) is a Canadian actor best known by his stage name Michael Ironside.
He has worked as a voice actor, producer, film director, and screenwriter in movie and television series in various Canadian and American productions.
He is best known for playing villains and "tough guy" heroes, though he has also portrayed sympathetic characters.
Ironside is a method actor, who stays in character between filming scenes.
British actor Christian Bale has starred in various films, as well as advertisements and a video game.
He made his acting debut in 1986, on the television film "".
The following year, he made his film debut starring alongside John Malkovich and Miranda Richardson in the war film "Empire of the Sun".
Bale's role of a young boy, interned in China by the Japanese, received praise from most film critics.
Two years later, Bale had a minor role in "Henry V", a drama film based on William Shakespeare's play "The Life of Henry the Fifth".
It has been considered one of the best Shakespeare film adaptations ever made.
In 1992, Bale starred as Jack Kelly in the Walt Disney musical drama "Newsies", which was a critical and commercial failure; however, it gained a cult following.
He received a role in the 1994 drama "Little Women", which garnered positive reviews.
Bale lent his voice for the Disney animated film "Pocahontas" in 1995, although it received a mixed reception and attained box office success.
He starred as British journalist Arthur Stuart in the Todd Haynes-directed drama "Velvet Goldmine" (1998).
Although critics were divided on the film, Bale's role was "eagerly anticipated".
Bale portrayed Demetrius in the critically praised 1999 film "A Midsummer Night's Dream", an adaptation of Shakespeare's play of the same name, directed by Michael Hoffman.
The same year, he portrayed Jesus of Nazareth in the television movie "Mary, Mother of Jesus".
Falling Awake (originally titled Shine On) is a 2009 musical drama film directed by Agustín, who co-wrote it with Michael Baez and Doug Klozzner.
Andrew Cisneros stars as a Latino musician in New York City who attempts to overcome the problems if his poor neighborhood.
It premiered at the New York International Latino Film Festival and was released in the US on January 29, 2010.
Rhythm (Tamil: ரிதம் ) is a 2000 Tamil musical drama film written and directed by Vasanth and produced by V. Natarajan.
The film stars Arjun, Jyothika, Ramesh Aravind and Meena in lead roles with Lakshmi, Nagesh and Manivannan in supporting ones.
The music is composed by A. R. Rahman, while cinematography was predominantly handled by P. S. Vinod and Sreekar Prasad edited the film.
The film was released in September 2000, receiving highly positive reviews from critics and became commercially successful.
The film's soundtrack by A. R. Rahman was also very successful and received a cult for musical drama films .
Nine is a 2009 romantic musical drama film directed and produced by Rob Marshall and written by Michael Tolkin and Anthony Minghella.
The film is an adaptation of the 1982 musical of the same name, which in turn is based on Federico Fellini's semi-autobiographical 1963 film "8½".
In addition to songs from the stage musical, all written by Maury Yeston, the film has three original songs, also written by Yeston.
The ensemble principal cast consists of Daniel Day-Lewis, Marion Cotillard, Penélope Cruz, Judi Dench, Fergie, Kate Hudson, Nicole Kidman, and Sophia Loren.
Dilip Kumar (born Muhammad Yusuf Khan; 11 December 1922) is an Indian film actor, producer and activist.
Also known as the "Tragedy King", he is credited with bringing realism to film acting since his first film which was released in 1944.
He is considered one of the greatest and most influential actors of all time.
He has been described as "the ultimate method actor" (natural actor) by Satyajit Ray.
Kumar is one of the biggest Indian movie stars, and a pioneer of method acting, predating Hollywood method actors such as Marlon Brando.
Kumar debuted as an actor in the film "Jwar Bhata" (1944), produced by Bombay Talkies.
His career has spanned over six decades and over 65 films.

Kumar is known for roles in films such as the romantic "Andaz" (1949), the heartwarming "Babul" (1950), the impassioned "Deedar" (1951), the swashbuckling "Aan" (1952), the dramatic "Devdas" (1955), the comical "Azaad" (1955), "Naya Daur" (1957), "Yahudi" (1958), "Madhumati" (1958), "Kohinoor" (1960), the epic historical "Mughal-e-Azam" (1960), the social "Gunga Jamuna" (1961) and "Ram Aur Shyam" (1967).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

hop1_docs: Dilip Kumar | Dilip Kumar (born Muhammad Yusuf Khan; 11 December 1922) is an Indian film actor, producer and activist.  Also known as the "Tragedy King", he is credited with bringing realism to film acting since his first film which was released in 1944.  He is considered one of the greatest and most influential actors of all time.  He has been described as "the ultimate method actor" (natural actor) by Satyajit Ray.  Kumar is one of the biggest Indian movie stars, and a pioneer of method acting, predating Hollywood method actors such as Marlon Brando.  Kumar debuted as an actor in the film "Jwar Bhata" (1944), produced by Bombay Talkies.  His career has spanned over six decades and over 65 films.  Kumar is known for roles in films such as the romantic "Andaz" (1949), the heartwarming "Babul" (1950), the impassioned "Deedar" (1951), the swashbuckling "Aan" (1952), the dramatic "Devdas" (1955), the comical "Azaad" (1955), "Naya Daur" (1957), "Yahudi" (1958), "Madhumati" (1958), "Kohinoor" (1960), the epic historical "Mughal-e-Azam" (1960), the social "Gunga Jamuna" (1961) and "Ram Aur Shyam" (1967).

Daniel Day-Lewis | Sir Daniel Michael Blake Day-Lewis (born 29 April 1957) is an English actor who holds both British and Irish citizenship.  Born and raised in London, he excelled on stage at the National Youth Theatre, before being accepted at the Bristol Old Vic Theatre School, which he attended for three years.  Despite his traditional actor training at the Bristol Old Vic, he is considered to be a method actor, known for his constant devotion to and research of his roles.  He would often remain completely in character for the duration of the shooting schedules of his films, even to the point of adversely affecting his health.  He is one of the most selective actors in the film industry, having starred in only five films since 1998, with as many as five years between roles.  Protective of his private life, he rarely gives interviews and makes very few public appearances.

Jim Sturgess | James Anthony Sturgess (born 16 May 1981) is an English actor and singer-songwriter.  His breakthrough role was appearing as Jude in the musical romance drama film "Across the Universe" (2007).  In 2008, he played the male lead role of Ben Campbell in "21".  In 2009, he played Gavin Kossef in the crime drama "Crossing Over", appearing with Harrison Ford, Ray Liotta and Ashley Judd.  In 2010, Sturgess starred in the film, "The Way Back", directed by Peter Weir.  Sturgess co-starred in the epic science fiction film "Cloud Atlas", which began filming in September 2011 and was released in October 2012.

Mike Norris (actor) | Michael R. "Mike" Norris (born October 4, 1962) is an American actor who has acted in over two dozen films.  He is the son of the more famous actor and martial arts champion Chuck Norris.  He starred in the 1986 drama film "Born American" and the 1991 action film "".  He also directed and starred in the 2009 Christian film, "Birdie & Bogey".

Michael Fassbender | Michael Fassbender (born 2 April 1977) is an Irish actor.  His feature film debut was in the fantasy war epic "300" (2007) as a Spartan warrior; his earlier roles included various stage productions, as well as starring roles on television such as in the HBO miniseries "Band of Brothers" (2001) and the Sky One fantasy drama "Hex" (2004–05).  He first came to prominence for his role as IRA activist Bobby Sands in "Hunger" (2008), for which he won a British Independent Film Award.  Subsequent roles include in the independent film "Fish Tank" (2009), as a Royal Marines lieutenant in "Inglourious Basterds" (2009), as Edward Rochester in the 2011 film adaptation of "Jane Eyre", as Carl Jung in "A Dangerous Method" (2011), as the sentient android David 8 in "Prometheus" (2012) and its sequel, "" (2017), and in the musical comedy-drama "Frank" (2014) as an eccentric musician loosely inspired by Frank Sidebottom.

ARY Film Award for Best Actor | The ARY Film Award for Best Actor is one of the ARY Film Awards of Merit presented annually by the ARY Digital Network and Entertainment Channel to male actor working in the film industry.  Best Actor is considered to be one of five most important awards of the ARY Film Awards, as it represents the main icon of cinematic industry on which all aspects of film such as directing, acting, music composing, writing, editing and other efforts that put forth into a drama is depending.  This award is one of the two Best Actor awards in ceremony, in which one is awarded to relevant film actor only by the decision of ceremony Jury, while other is being awarded on Viewers Voting's.

To-Day | To-Day is a 1917 silent film drama directed by Ralph Ince and starring Florence Reed.  A story about prostitution, this film is based on a 1913 stage play,"Today", by George Broadhurst and Abraham S. Schomer and starred Emily Stevens which ran for an astounding 280 performances in eight months time.  Actors Gus Weinburg and Alice Gale are the only actors in the film that appeared in the play.  It is considered to be a lost film.
hop1_titles: ARY Film Award for Best Actor
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Daniel Day-Lewis — (passage "Daniel Day-Lewis"): "born 29 April 1957"; "is an English actor ... considered to be a method actor".
  - Dilip Kumar — (passage "Dilip Kumar"): "born 11 December 1922"; "described as 'the ultimate method actor'" (not English, wrong birth year).
  - Jim Sturgess — (passage "Jim Sturgess"): "born 16 May 1981" (not 1957).
  - No passage explicitly mentions the film "Nine (2009)" or links any of the above actors to that film.
- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Missing explicit credit: which actor(s) "starred in the 2009 musical drama film 'Nine'" is not present in the passages.
  - Potential name overlap: multiple actors are described as method actors historically (e.g., Dilip Kumar) but Dilip Kumar is not English/1957 — this could be a distractor.
- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Confirmation that Daniel Day-Lewis appears in (or starred in) Nine (2009), or alternatively the film's credited lead actor(s)/cast list.
- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. Retrieve the "Nine (2009 film)" page (cast list and starring credits).
  2. Retrieve "Daniel Day-Lewis filmography" or the "Daniel Day-Lewis" page section listing films/cast.
  3. Search query: "Daniel Day-Lewis Nine 2009 cast" or "Nine 2009 cast list".
hop2_docs: Nine (2009 live-action film) | Nine is a 2009 romantic musical drama film directed and produced by Rob Marshall and written by Michael Tolkin and Anthony Minghella.  The film is an adaptation of the 1982 musical of the same name, which in turn is based on Federico Fellini's semi-autobiographical 1963 film "8½".  In addition to songs from the stage musical, all written by Maury Yeston, the film has three original songs, also written by Yeston.  The ensemble principal cast consists of Daniel Day-Lewis, Marion Cotillard, Penélope Cruz, Judi Dench, Fergie, Kate Hudson, Nicole Kidman, and Sophia Loren.

The Boxer (1997 film) | The Boxer is a 1997 sports-drama film by Irish director Jim Sheridan.  Starring Daniel Day-Lewis and Emily Watson, the film centers on the life of a boxer and former Provisional IRA volunteer Danny Flynn, played by Day-Lewis, who is trying to "go straight" after his release from prison.  The film is the third collaboration between Sheridan and Day-Lewis, and portrays the increase of splinter groups within the IRA.

Phantom Thread | Phantom Thread is an upcoming American drama film, set in London's fashion world in the 1950s, shot, written and directed by Paul Thomas Anderson.  The film stars Daniel Day-Lewis, Lesley Manville, Richard Graham, Fraser Delaney and Vicky Krieps.  It is reportedly Daniel Day-Lewis's final performance in a film, following four decades in the profession.  The music will be composed by longtime Anderson collaborator and Radiohead member Jonny Greenwood.  The film is set to be released on December 25, 2017.
List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Day-Lewis | List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Day-Lewis

Tamasin Day-Lewis | Lydia Tamasin Day-Lewis (born 17 September 1953) is an English television chef and food critic.  Day-Lewis is the daughter of the poet Cecil Day-Lewis and actress Jill Balcon and the sister of the actor Daniel Day-Lewis.

There Will Be Blood | There Will Be Blood is a 2007 American drama film written and directed by Paul Thomas Anderson.  It stars Daniel Day-Lewis and Paul Dano.  The film was inspired by Upton Sinclair's novel "Oil! ".  It tells the story of a silver miner-turned-oilman (Day-Lewis) on a ruthless quest for wealth during Southern California's oil boom of the late 19th and early 20th centuries.  Kevin J. O'Connor, Ciarán Hinds, and Dillon Freasier are also featured in the film.

Cecil Day-Lewis | Cecil Day-Lewis (or Day Lewis) (27 April 1904 – 22 May 1972), often writing as C. Day-Lewis, was an Anglo-Irish poet and the Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom from 1968 until his death in 1972.  He also wrote mystery stories under the pseudonym of Nicholas Blake.  He was the father of actor Daniel Day-Lewis and documentary filmmaker and television chef Tamasin Day-Lewis.

response.summary_2: Daniel Day-Lewis (born 29 April 1957; English actor considered to be a method actor). He is listed among the ensemble principal cast of the 2009 film Nine.
